brand_description: Midnight Province is an original AI-assisted artist brand for minimalist dark-pop, art-pop, synth-pop, and emotionally sharp coming-of-age songs. The sound blends sparse drums, intimate low-register vocals, suburban-night imagery, elegant restraint, explosive emotional choruses, and cinematic youth-to-adult tension. It should feel cool, private, observant, wounded, stylish, and quietly intense without imitating any real artist, voice, cadence, lyrics, melodies, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Listeners who like minimalist dark-pop, art-pop, synth-pop, moody alt-pop, intimate vocal performances, sharp coming-of-age writing, late-night emotional detail, and songs that feel restrained, elegant, and emotionally devastating without being overproduced.
audience.guardrail: Suitable for general streaming audiences ages 16+. Allow romance, jealousy, loneliness, partying, status anxiety, heartbreak, growing up, friendship tension, and emotional volatility. Avoid explicit sexual detail, graphic violence, hate, slurs, self-harm instruction, drug-use instruction, or child-directed framing.

character.name: Midnight Province
character.core_concept: A private pop narrator watching parties, bedrooms, highways, and friendships from the edge of the room, turning small social details into huge emotional weather.
character.fallback_summary: minimalist dark-pop artist blending intimate vocals, sparse production, suburban-night detail, and cinematic emotional release
personality_traits: Private and restrained until the chorus breaks open
Romantic, jealous, lonely, and self-aware in different lanes
Able to make ordinary rooms feel cinematic
Stylish and minimal without losing warmth
character.visual_identity: Minimal dark-pop visuals with night roads, pool light, mirror glass, deep blue shadows, pale gold highlights, empty rooms after parties, suburban streetlights, and editorial typography. Visuals should feel intimate, cinematic, expensive-but-understated, and emotionally charged.
visual_reference: Deep navy, black, pale gold, wine red, soft cream, and sodium streetlight palette
Empty party rooms, parking lots, bedrooms, mirrors, pools, highways, and night windows used occasionally, not forced
Minimal editorial cover design with large negative space
Subtle grain, soft flash photography feeling, and cinematic blue-hour lighting
No celebrity likeness, no copied album layouts, no dense typography

music.default_style: minimalist dark-pop and art-pop with sparse drums, synth bass, intimate vocals, cinematic pads, and explosive emotional chorus options
music.default_prompt: Original minimalist dark-pop with sparse programmed drums, deep synth bass, intimate low-register vocal presence, elegant negative space, soft harmonies, cinematic pads, and carefully placed emotional lift. Vary the lane by song: cool restrained dark-pop, glittering heartbreak anthem, sparse bedroom confession, party-afterglow song, anxious synth-pop pulse, intimate piano/synth ballad, and explosive final-chorus catharsis are all valid. Never imitate, clone, or reference any real artist voice, vocal tone, cadence, lyrics, melody, beat, arrangement, production, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with restraint, emotional precision, youth-memory detail, elegance, and melodic impact.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: private jealousy, friendship drift, first adult heartbreak, parties that feel lonely, status and class observation, suburban nights, self-mythology, quiet confidence, romantic obsession, growing up too fast, and the moment after a huge emotional event. Do not repeat the same crown, royal, party, glass, room, blue, youth, gold, or angel imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a precise final image, whispered realization, unresolved emotional turn, repeated soft phrase, or cinematic cut-to-black; avoid tidy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original pop songs that feel restrained, intelligent, intimate, stylish, and emotionally huge, with enough variation to support albums without repeating one sparse-pop formula.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Mostly first-person and direct second-person, with occasional observer distance. The narrator can be cool, jealous, heartbroken, detached, euphoric, embarrassed, or quietly powerful depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person or second-person, with occasional cinematic observer framing.
character_voice.tone: Intimate, cool, dry, emotionally precise, restrained, dramatic under the surface, and never derivative.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plain modern language with sharp social detail, small luxury or suburban images, emotional understatement, and memorable concrete nouns used sparingly.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Very light dry wit or social observation only; avoid novelty or joke-song framing.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

reference_artists_for_internal_vibe_only: Lorde Pure Heroine-era minimalist dark-pop coolness
Lorde Melodrama-era emotional maximalism inside elegant pop
songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, vocal tone, cadence, phrasing, lyrics, melodies, drum programming, synth motifs, arrangements, album sequencing, persona, or copyrighted material. Use broad emotional and sonic traits—minimalism, intimacy, observational writing, controlled cool, and explosive emotional release—as inspiration for an original Midnight Province identity.
allowed_elements: sparse programmed drums and negative space
intimate low-register vocal energy without soundalike imitation
deep synth bass, soft pads, and restrained percussion
sharp social observation and small room details
coming-of-age romance, jealousy, friendship, and status tension
explosive chorus lift after restrained verses
party imagery with loneliness or self-awareness underneath
plain emotional lines surrounded by cinematic imagery
clean or radio-friendly language by default
subtle harmony stacks and chant-like refrains used sparingly
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist vocal style, cadence, melody, beat, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
overusing royal, crown, party, blue, glass, youth, or invented lore imagery
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, production lane, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be memorable and emotionally precise, but every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete room, clothing, light, weather, social, or body-language detail in each song.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be sung or spoken.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Avoid explicit content unless a separate release profile explicitly permits it.
Emotional darkness is allowed, but the song must not instruct or encourage self-harm, dangerous behavior, or substance abuse.
structure_preferences: [COLD VOCAL or TEXTURE] -> [VERSE 1] -> [PRE-HOOK] -> [CHORUS] -> [VERSE 2] -> [CHORUS] -> [BRIDGE or QUIET TURN] -> [FINAL CHORUS] -> [OUTRO]
song_family_types: Minimal dark-pop statement with sparse drums, cool vocal restraint, and sharp social detail.
Glittering heartbreak anthem with bigger chorus, emotional lift, and cinematic synths.
Bedroom confession with close vocal, minimal bass, and a devastating plainspoken hook.
Afterparty song with lonely celebration, pulsing beat, and bittersweet crowd imagery.
Anxious synth-pop track with faster pulse, clipped phrasing, and status tension.
Piano/synth ballad with understated vocal, slow build, and quiet final image.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate drum feel: minimal kick/snare, dry clap pattern, pulsing synth-pop beat, half-time emotional stomp, near-beatless ballad, or late-night dance pulse.
Rotate bass role: deep synth sub, melodic bass hook, sparse pulsing low-end, warm electric bass support, distorted synth growl, or no bass until chorus.
Rotate vocal shape: close low lead, whispered double, restrained verse, chant-like refrain, stacked final chorus, or exposed bridge vocal.
Rotate texture: empty-room minimalism, glossy heartbreak pop, blue-hour synth haze, dry bedroom demo feel, cinematic club afterglow, or piano-led intimacy.
Use big chorus lifts selectively; some songs should stay small and tense by design.
tempo_energy_lanes: 62-76 BPM: slow piano/synth confession, sparse and emotionally exposed.
77-90 BPM: restrained dark-pop groove, minimal drums, cool vocal control.
91-104 BPM: pulsing art-pop lane, balanced hook and verse energy.
105-118 BPM: brighter synth-pop movement, anxious party or heartbreak energy.
119-128 BPM: rare dance-adjacent catharsis, used for emotional release rather than default.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: cold vocal, dry drum hit, synth pulse, bass note, room tone, piano figure, or immediate lyric image.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, drum feel, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, structure, or production density.
Do not always build to a huge final chorus; sometimes end small, dry, whispered, or abruptly unresolved.
Use silence and negative space as arrangement features, not absence of effort.
Avoid identical verse lengths, chorus density, bridge placement, and final-hook stacking across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: understated refrain, explosive chorus, one-line mantra, whispered callback, chant-like phrase, or instrumental synth motif.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, vocal register, or phrase shape across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: jealousy, release, numbness, awe, regret, power, loneliness, or surrender.
Use repetition for emotional gravity, but change harmony, silence, texture, lyric detail, or backing vocal shape on later returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: private confession, direct romantic address, observer at a party, friendship postmortem, status critique, self-mythologizing narrator, or quiet morning-after reflection.
Do not make every song about parties, royalty, youth, or heartbreak; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics are cool or detached, include one line that reveals the wound underneath.
When lyrics are romantic, include imperfection, jealousy, distance, or social pressure rather than generic sweetness.
When lyrics are dramatic, ground them in one ordinary object or room detail.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same crown, royal, glass, party, blue, gold, room, angel, youth, or car imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, symbols, objects, slogans, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as arriving at a party, staring in a mirror, driving at night, or texting an ex.
Do not reuse the same chorus cadence, drum pattern, synth motif, emotional reveal, or final-image style across multiple tracks.
Keep Midnight Province identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible minimal-pop track that establishes voice, space, and emotional intelligence without using the biggest chorus first.
Sequence sparse songs beside bigger synth-pop or heartbreak anthems so the album breathes.
Use afterparty or dance-adjacent songs as emotional contrast, not pure celebration.
Place the most vulnerable ballad after a cooler or more observational track.
Save one maximal emotional chorus for the late-middle or final third.
End with either a small unresolved image, a quiet confession, or a final chorus that feels like lights coming on after the party.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable vocal phrase, synth pulse, bass movement, or lyric image within the first 12 seconds.
Pair restrained verses with a clearer emotional hook.
Let negative space, dry drums, and small melodic changes create replay value.
Use one vivid object or social detail per section rather than crowding the lyric with symbols.
Keep hooks memorable without making them childish or over-explained.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be emotionally precise, minimal, chantable, or quietly explosive.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse restraint and chorus emotional scale.
For sparse lanes, let silence and phrasing carry the hook; for anthem lanes, let melody and stacked vocals carry it.

energy_arc.pattern: GLOW -> OBSERVE -> ACHE -> LIFT -> BREAK -> AFTERLIGHT
rules: Start with a recognizable vocal, drum, bass, synth, or room-tone identity rather than a long empty intro.
Build intimacy through detail and restraint before the first emotional lift.
Reveal the plain emotional truth before the final third.
Let the final section shift energy: bigger, smaller, stranger, colder, or more euphoric depending on the song family.
End with a memorable afterlight rather than a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Soft repeated refrain listeners can sing without dense lyrics
One-line chant used sparingly for emotional release
Background vocal answer that feels like inner thought
Instrumental synth motif that returns like a memory
Final lyric tag that reframes the narrator's coolness
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Lorde imitation rather than an original adjacent dark-pop/art-pop lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, drum feel, bass role, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production density.
If a song is minimal, confirm it still has a memorable hook, image, or vocal motif.
If a song is dramatic, confirm it has concrete detail and not just abstract emotion.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: Midnight Province's vocal is irreplaceable because it delivers enormous emotional stakes at near-conversational volume, using flatness and plain speech rhythm to make a single held note or a dropped syllable feel like a confession being dragged out of someone who didn't want to confess.
vocal_textures: A low-chest, slightly dry speaking-register delivery in verses — not breathy, not polished, almost like thinking out loud into a microphone placed too close
Deliberate syllable compression where multi-syllable words are flattened into one rhythmic unit, giving lyrics the feel of a text message read aloud
A sudden leap into pure-tone head voice on a single word inside a chorus — isolated, slightly cold, not melismatic, like a light turning on in a dark room
Backing harmonies stacked close in pitch, parallel and slightly emotionless, functioning more as timbral widening than as melodic decoration
A controlled near-mono dynamic in verses with volume swell saved exclusively for the one line in the chorus that earns it
Occasional deliberate roughness at phrase endings — not as vocal fry affectation but as honest breath running out
timing_behaviors: Placing lyric phrases slightly behind the beat in verses as if the narrator is reluctant to arrive at the word they need most
Landing chorus hook words exactly on the downbeat with no anticipation, creating a sudden weight after verse drift
Stretching monosyllabic words — especially pronouns and prepositions — across beat divisions to make ordinary grammar feel loaded
Using hard rhythmic chops on short declarative phrases in bridge sections, clipped and metronomic, as if forcing clarity after emotional ambiguity
Allowing a breath gap before the final chorus hook rather than connecting it to the previous phrase, letting silence act as punctuation
adlib_behaviors: A near-inaudible repetition of the last word of a hook phrase, mixed under the lead vocal, heard more as resonance than as a separate take
Single-word breath vocals — 'oh', 'yeah', or a wordless exhale — placed at the end of a section rather than inside it, functioning as a period not an exclamation
Harmony ad-libs that diverge from the lead melody by no more than a minor third, keeping the emotional texture cool rather than gospel-wide
Occasional spoken fragment buried in the left channel during a bridge — one phrase, no reverb, as if overheard rather than performed
Silence used as the adlib: a held space after the hook where a fill or ad-lib would be expected, creating absence as emphasis
avoid: Any riffing, melisma, or vocal run — this character does not ornament; every note is chosen because it is the only one
Bright, forward, cheerleader-register delivery — the vocal lives in the lower half of the chest and never performs happiness it does not mean
Oversaturated reverb that makes the voice feel large or arena-scaled — intimate close-mic dryness is the default even when the production is cinematic
Lyric phrasing that follows an obvious pop cadence or call-and-response structure — this voice interrupts the expected rhythm rather than fulfilling it
Emotional generosity in delivery — when the lyric is devastating, the vocal should be cooler than expected, not more expressive

performance_conceit_bank: The voice delivers the most emotionally loaded line of the song at the lowest dynamic level on the track — the chorus drops in volume while the production lifts, making the listener lean in rather than pull back.

A verse lyric is delivered in near-monotone rhythmic speech for two lines, then the third line breaks into melody without warning — the melodic arrival is the emotional event, not a musical expectation.
The title phrase of the song is sung only once, on its first appearance, with no repetition — its hook power comes from restraint and precise placement rather than repetition across the track.

A harmony enters one beat early on the second chorus, slightly displacing the familiar version of the hook, creating a sense that something has shifted in the narrator's emotional position since the first chorus.

The pre-chorus drops all percussion and bass for exactly two bars, leaving voice and a single synth pad — the beat returns not on a downbeat but one 16th note late, making the chorus arrival feel like stumbling forward.

An entire verse is performed without any vibrato or pitch sustain — completely flat and spoken in rhythm — so that the first note held in the hook carries all the feeling accumulated across the verse.
The outro repeats the hook melody but removes one word each time it cycles, so the final iteration is a single syllable or a breath — the song dissolves rather than resolves.

In a bridge section, the lead vocal is doubled by a version of itself panned opposite and pitched down a minor third with no reverb, giving the narrator the quality of arguing with a calmer version of themselves.

album_mode_lanes.name: Provincial Cool
album_mode_lanes.description: Sparse dark-pop tracks where Midnight Province narrates social environments — parties, parking lots, group chats — from a position of deliberate distance. Production is minimal: one synth texture, dry programmed drums, a bass note that barely moves. The vocal is unhurried and slightly superior. This is the character watching rather than participating.

album_mode_lanes.name: Plain Wound
album_mode_lanes.description: Bedroom-scale confessional tracks where the production strips down to a piano or single synth and the vocal carries the entire emotional argument. No chorus lift, no cathartic build — just a plainspoken statement delivered at a level that makes it feel like something the narrator would never say out loud. These tracks end before the resolution.

album_mode_lanes.name: Glitter Damage
album_mode_lanes.description: Heartbreak tracks with full cinematic production — stacked synths, bigger programmed drums, chorus lift — but where the emotional content is specific, social, and embarrassing rather than universal. The narrator isn't destroyed; they're mortified. Production brightness and lyric precision are in intentional contrast.

album_mode_lanes.name: Afterglow Static
album_mode_lanes.description: Post-event songs set in the hour after a party, a fight, a kiss, or a decision. Production uses slow pulses, faint crowd or room-tone textures, and a vocal that sounds slightly hoarse or tired. These are neither sad nor happy — they are the specific texture of transition between one state and another.

album_mode_lanes.name: Anxious Pulse
album_mode_lanes.description: Higher-BPM synth-pop tracks where the drum pattern is tight and slightly relentless, the bass is clipped, and the vocal is clipped and metronomic in the verse — status anxiety, social performance, the sensation of being watched. The hook releases the tension exactly once and does not repeat it comfortably.

album_mode_lanes.name: Cold Ceremony
album_mode_lanes.description: Slow, formal tracks that treat an ordinary emotional event — a conversation ending, someone leaving a room, deciding not to call — with the production weight of a ceremony. Piano or synth pads, no drums or very slow kick only, long vocal phrases with significant silence between them. Emotional scale is enormous; sonic scale is minimal.

song_differentiation_rules: If the prior track used a slow BPM, spoken-register verse delivery, and a minimal chorus drop in volume, the next track must change at least two of those three: raise the BPM, introduce a melodic verse, or allow the chorus to genuinely expand in production density.

If the prior track was narrated from observer distance — watching a party, describing others — the next track must shift to a direct intimate address or a first-person vulnerability that removes the narrator's coolness as a shield.

If the prior track ended small, whispered, or unresolved, the following track must establish its emotional position clearly within the first 20 seconds rather than building ambiguity again from silence.

No two consecutive tracks should use the same intro type: if one begins with a cold vocal, the next must begin with a drum hit, bass note, synth pulse, or immediate lyric image — not another cold vocal over nothing.

If the prior track's hook used understatement and flat delivery as its primary device, the next hook must use melody, stack, or volume contrast as its primary device — the restraint conceit cannot be the default tool on every song.

If the prior track's central image was drawn from a social environment — a party, a school hallway, a group — the next track must anchor its central image in something private: a body, a room alone, a domestic object, or an interior sensation.
anti_generic_rules: Do not default to a slow minor-key verse followed by a swelling synth chorus every time emotional scale is required — Midnight Province earns scale through accumulation of plain detail, not through production volume alone.

Do not write hooks that are emotionally generalized or universal in the way of standard pop — every hook must contain one specific social, physical, or situational detail that could only belong to this narrator's particular experience.
Do not use the sparse production as an excuse for lyric vagueness — the fewer sounds on the track, the more precise and concrete each lyric line must be.
Do not make the narrator wise, healed, or instructional — Midnight Province is watching and feeling, not teaching. Remove any lyric that sounds like advice, resolution, or hard-won lesson.
Do not allow the emotional climax to be the loudest moment by default — the most devastating delivery on the track should often be the quietest, the driest, or the most suddenly unadorned.
Do not repeat the social-observer framing for more than two songs on any project without introducing a track where the narrator is the one being watched, exposed, or embarrassed.

Do not write pre-choruses that simply raise energy and promise the chorus — Midnight Province pre-choruses must introduce a detail, a doubt, or a slight swerve in the narrator's position that reframes what the chorus means.

Do not pad out tracks with instrumental outros, repeated hooks over fading production, or generic eight-bar breakdowns — every section must earn its presence with a distinct lyric, melodic, or textural idea.
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use a slow descending synth pad as the primary atmospheric texture in more than two tracks — rotate to dry piano, clipped synth pulse, room tone, or bass-only negative space.
Do not use the near-monotone speaking-register verse delivery as the default mode — it is a specific tool for specific tracks, not the Midnight Province verse style by default.
Do not place the emotional revelation in the bridge on every track — rotate where the truth arrives: end of verse, pre-chorus undercut, post-chorus drop, or final line with no follow-up.
Do not anchor every song's central image in a social gathering or its aftermath — rotate to domestic private spaces, bodies, weather, transit, or the specific silence of a phone not ringing.
Do not stack close harmonies on the chorus of every track — in some tracks the lead vocal must be completely alone in the mix at maximum exposure.
Do not use a sudden final chorus key change, production swell, or added drum element as the climax device on more than one track per project.
Do not write every outro as a fading repetition of the hook — rotate to abrupt cut, single dry line with no music, held note dissolving, or a new melodic idea that does not repeat the chorus.
Do not give every track a four-bar percussion-free intro followed by bass entry at bar five — vary the opening moment radically from track to track so no two songs announce themselves the same way.
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify a concrete social or physical setting unique to this track — a specific type of room, event, or moment — and explain how that setting shapes both the vocal delivery register and the central lyric image, so the song is not set in a generic emotional space.

The brief must describe the exact point in the track's structure where the narrator's emotional position shifts or cracks, and must explain why that moment is placed structurally where it is rather than at the conventional bridge or final chorus.

The brief must specify how the silence and negative space on this track functions differently from the silence on any adjacent track in the project — naming what the silence means emotionally for this narrator in this song.

The brief must identify the one lyric line that carries the track's entire emotional argument and confirm that no other line in the song competes with it for that function, ensuring the track has a single precise center of gravity.

The brief must describe the vocal delivery mode for this track's verses — specifying whether it is speech-rhythm flat, melodic, compressed, or delayed — and confirm it differs from the verse delivery mode of the preceding track.

The brief must specify what the narrator knows at the end of the song that they did not know or admit at the start, and confirm that this shift is communicated through lyric detail or structural choice rather than through an explicit statement in the lyric.

visuals.style: Minimal cinematic dark-pop cover art with deep night colors, blue-hour lighting, empty rooms, pool glow, streetlights, mirror reflections, soft grain, and clean editorial typography. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit sex imagery, gore, weapons as focal point, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: minimal editorial pop typography, limited words, deep navy/gold contrast, elegant spacing, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; heartbreak, partying, jealousy, and lightly mature themes allowed without explicit content
